title: Writing - Part 1: University Education
description: Your class has been discussing whether everyone should go to university.

content.question: Write an essay discussing two of the arguments in your notes. You should explain which argument you find most convincing, giving reasons for your opinion.

arguments: Better career opportunities and higher salaries
The high cost of tuition fees and student debt
Developing critical thinking and academic knowledge
opinions: A university degree is no longer a guarantee of a good job.
The financial burden of student debt can last for decades.
Vocational training is a more practical choice for many young people.

structure.introduction: Introduce the debate about university education and the two arguments you will discuss.
structure.bodyParagraph1: Discuss the first argument (e.g., better career opportunities) and why many people see university as worthwhile.
structure.bodyParagraph2: Discuss the second argument (e.g., high cost and debt) and why some question the value of a degree.
structure.conclusion: Evaluate both arguments and state which you find most convincing.

content.modelAnswer:
The question of whether every young person should attend university is one that sparks lively debate in many countries. While higher education offers clear advantages, its rising cost has led many to question whether it represents good value for money. This essay will consider the career benefits of a degree and the serious issue of student debt.

A university degree continues to open doors that might otherwise remain closed. Graduates typically have access to a wider range of career options and tend to earn more over their lifetimes than those without degrees. Many professions, including law, medicine and engineering, require a degree as a minimum qualification. For ambitious young people with clear career goals, university remains an important and worthwhile investment.

However, the financial cost of university has risen sharply in many countries, leaving graduates with significant levels of debt. Some students spend years repaying loans before they can start saving or investing in their futures. For those who study subjects with limited job prospects or who do not complete their degrees, the financial burden can outweigh the benefits considerably.

In conclusion, while the career advantages of a degree are real, I believe the problem of student debt is the more convincing argument against making university the default choice for all young people. Society should do more to promote alternative routes, such as apprenticeships and vocational training, which can offer equally fulfilling and well-paid careers without the financial burden.
